mnemonics.title: Sum vs Product Rule
mnemonics.content: 'SUM for Separate, PRODUCT for Sequential' - Use SUM when choosing one OR the other (mutually exclusive). Use PRODUCT when doing one AND THEN another (sequential).

mnemonics.title: Permutation vs Combination
mnemonics.content: 'P for Position (order matters), C for Committee (order doesn't matter)' - Permutations = arrangements, Combinations = selections.

mnemonics.title: nCr Formula
mnemonics.content: 'Coke equals nFactorial over rFactorial times nMinus rFactorial' - nCr = n!/(r!(n-r)!)

mnemonics.title: Pigeonhole Principle
mnemonics.content: 'More items than boxes means at least one box has multiple items' - n > m guarantees at least one container has ⌈n/m⌉ or more items.

acronyms.expansion: Fundamental Counting Principle - multiply choices for each step
acronyms.usage: When making sequential independent choices, multiply the number of ways at each step.
P for ordered arrangements, C for unordered selections.

acronyms.expansion: Stars and Bars - formula for distributing identical items
acronyms.usage: Distributing n identical items into r distinct boxes: (n+r-1)C(r-1) ways.

visualTips.visualization: Draw two separate circles representing two sets of choices with no overlap. The total is the sum of both circles.
Draw a tree diagram branching from first choice to second choice. Each branch multiplies to get total outcomes.
Think of slots: _ _ _ with n options for first, (n-1) for second, etc. Multiply n × (n-1) × (n-2)...
Think of selecting a team where order doesn't matter. Take permutations and divide by r! to remove order arrangements.
Imagine n pigeons trying to fit into m holes where n > m. Some hole must have more than one pigeon.

analogies.analogy: Choosing between pizza OR burger at a restaurant - you can only pick one, total options = options in A + options in B.
Getting dressed - you have 3 shirts AND 4 pants. Each shirt can be paired with each pant. Total = 3 × 4 = 12 outfits.
Finishing positions in a race - 1st, 2nd, 3rd place matter. Different orderings create different results.
Selecting committee members - whether Alice is chosen first or last doesn't matter, only that she's on the committee.
In a class of 13 students, at least 2 must share a birth month since there are only 12 months.
